title: Mud on Denim
stainChemistry: Mud contains mineral particles (clay, silt, sand), organic matter (decomposed plant material), and water. On denim, the clay particles bond electrostatically to fiber and the organic matter stains through tannin-like mechanisms. Wet mud is actually harder to remove than dry mud — water causes clay particles to penetrate deeper. Dry mud brushes off relatively easily.
whyThisWorks: Let the mud dry completely on denim first — this is counterintuitive but critical. Dry mud loses its cohesion and clay particles release from the fiber surface. Brushing and vacuuming removes the majority of dry mud mechanically. Surfactant then addresses the remaining organic discoloration. For clay-heavy mud, a mild acid rinse helps release electrostatically-bound particles.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Remove loose material mechanically — brush, scrape, or vacuum as appropriate
2. Apply NSD to the stain area — 5-10 min dwell time
3. Apply appropriate secondary agent based on stain response — 5-10 min dwell time
4. Flush with cool water. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse — 1-2 min
warnings: Always test on hidden area first

steps: 1. Let the mud dry COMPLETELY — this is counterintuitive but critical
2. Brush off dried mud. Vacuum remaining particles
3. Apply Dawn dish soap mixed with cold water for remaining discoloration — 5-10 min dwell time
4. Blot with clean white cloth. Rinse with cold water
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional for specialized treatment
warnings: Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first
Air dry until stain is confirmed gone — heat sets most stains

neverDo: No chlorine bleach — removes indigo unevenly
No strong detergent on raw denim
fiberSensitivities: Indigo dye sits on surface — aggressive treatment causes uneven fading
Washed denim is more forgiving than raw

escalation.when: If mud stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is mud on denim, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
